title: Vomit on Cotton
stainChemistry: Vomit is a complex mixture: partially digested food (protein + fat + starch), stomach acid (HCl, pH 1.5–3.5), bile salts (natural surfactants), mucus, and live bacteria. The acid can strip dye or weaken cotton if left in contact — cotton itself tolerates acid at neutral strength but the localized low pH damages some reactive dyes. Protein component cooks into the fiber under heat. Bile salts act as surfactants that drive the stain deeper. Odor comes from volatile fatty acids + bacterial metabolism.
whyThisWorks: Remove solids first to stop acid contact + get the mass off before chemistry. Cold flush dilutes the acid and moves soluble protein before heat sets it. Enzyme digests the food-protein component. Dilute baking-soda or NSD rinse neutralizes the residual acid. Hot wash kills bacteria + finishes. Order matters: solids → cold flush → enzyme → neutralize → hot wash. Hot wash before enzyme cooks the protein in; enzyme before acid-neutralization leaves residual low pH that can damage dye over hours.
defaultAssumption: Treat with gloves — vomit is biohazard-adjacent. Fresh vomit on cotton is solvable but the odor requires the hot-wash finisher. Kids' / sickness vomit on bedding is the typical case; mattress involvement escalates to a carpet/upholstery pro.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Disposable gloves, dull blade, dedicated towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on. Scrape solid material off with a dull blade onto a disposable surface — newspaper or a paper towel you can throw away. Don't rub. Dispose of the scraped material in a sealed bag.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from the back, dilute acid
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Blot from underneath. Cold only — warm water cooks the protein AND accelerates the acid's dye damage. Flushing also dilutes the stomach acid to safer pH before it sits on the fabric for hours.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease digestant) in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Protein Spotter or enzyme pretreat dissolved in warm water. Protease digests the food-protein matrix + any mucus. Dwell 20–30 min — longer than milk because vomit has more protein substrate. Light brush after the first 10.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp baking soda in 1 cup cool water
spottingProtocol.instruction: After the enzyme, neutralize any remaining stomach acid with a dilute baking-soda rinse. Mild alkalinity counters the HCl. This step protects reactive dyes from long-term fading. Rinse cool.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. Emulsifies residual food oils and bile-salt film.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, outdoor line if available
spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder hot per care label — kills residual bacteria that cause persistent odor. Dry in direct sunlight when possible; UV is a second antimicrobial pass.

steps: 2. Cold flush from the back — dilutes stomach acid, moves soluble protein.
3. Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease) in warm water (30–38°C). Dwell 20–30 min.
5. NSD — emulsify residual food oils and bile salts. Cool rinse.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease digestant)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush; warm (30–38°C) for enzyme; cool through neutralize + NSD; hot wash post-chemistry; sun during dry
warnings: Never warm-flush vomit — cooks the protein + accelerates dye damage
Hot wash is non-negotiable on vomit — kills bacteria, clears odor

homeSolutions.instruction: Put on rubber or nitrile gloves and use a towel you can toss (not a favorite) to handle the fabric. Vomit is biohazard-adjacent — gloves for your own health, separate towel to avoid cross-contaminating clean laundry.

Take the fabric outside or over a trash bin. Scrape solid material off with a dull butter knife or plastic scraper onto newspaper or a paper towel you can throw away. Seal in a bag. Don't rub — scraping lifts, rubbing grinds into the weave.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water, Flush From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for 1–2 minutes. The flush dilutes the stomach acid AND pushes the food/protein back the way it came in. Cold only — warm water cooks the protein in and can damage the cotton's dye.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Warm Water, Longer Dwell
homeSolutions.instruction: Use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout). Dissolve in WARM water per the label. Apply to the stain and let it sit 20–30 minutes — longer than usual because vomit has more protein substrate to digest.

homeSolutions.agent: Baking Soda Rinse for Acid + Odor
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon baking soda into 1 cup cool water. Sponge onto the spot, let sit 5 minutes, rinse. The baking soda neutralizes any remaining stomach acid (protecting your cotton's dye from long-term fading) AND cuts odor. This step is often the difference between 'cleaned but still faintly smells' and 'fully clean.'
A drop of Dawn worked in gently with a soft toothbrush lifts any residual food oils and bile-salt film. Rinse cool.

Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. The hot wash is what kills the bacteria that cause persistent vomit odor. Dry in direct sunlight when possible — UV is a second kill. If you must machine-dry, hot dryer is fine after the enzyme + hot-wash has cleared the protein.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Mattress or Persistent Odor
homeSolutions.instruction: If vomit soaked into a mattress, carpet pad, or upholstery batting, home treatment can't reach the trapped material and bacteria will continue producing odor. Call a professional cleaner with hot-water extraction. Tell them: "Vomit on cotton [mattress cover / bedding / garment / upholstery], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + baking soda + Dawn + hot wash. [Odor persists / stain clear]."

steps: 1. Gloves on. Separate towel. Outdoors if you can.
2. Scrape solids onto disposable paper and bag-seal.
3. Cold flush from the BACK — dilutes stomach acid + moves protein.
4. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Zout) in WARM water. 20–30 min.
5. Baking soda + water rinse for acid + odor.
6. Dawn + soft brush for food oils.
7. Hot wash + sunlight dry — kills bacteria.
8. Escalate for mattress / upholstery / persistent odor after full cycle.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop home treatment if vomit soaked into a mattress or upholstery padding — the bacteria trap requires professional extraction. Also escalate if odor persists after a full cycle.
warnings: Cold water for first flush — warm cooks protein + damages cotton dye
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm not hot
Hot wash is the bacterial-kill — cold wash leaves odor alive

neverDo: Never warm-water-flush fresh vomit — cooks protein + accelerates dye damage from the acid
Never skip the hot wash — bacterial odor returns
Never skip the neutralize step — residual stomach acid continues to fade dye over hours
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, baking-soda rinse, NSD, hot wash
Dyed cotton: the stomach-acid-on-dye issue is the risk — neutralize quickly

materialWarnings: Cold during spotting — warm cooks protein + damages cotton dye
Hot wash + sunlight dry is the odor-kill

escalation.when: Vomit soaked into a mattress, carpet pad, or upholstery batting; persistent odor after a full home cycle; or the cotton has visible dye damage from acid contact.
escalation.whatToTell: "Vomit on [cotton garment / mattress cover / bedding / upholstery], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + baking-soda rinse + Dawn + hot wash. [Odor persists / stain clear / dye damage visible.]"
escalation.specialistType: professional cleaner with hot-water extraction (carpet / upholstery specialist if padding involved)

contextQuestions: Has the vomit soaked into a mattress, sofa batting, or carpet padding?
How long ago did it happen?
Has it been through any wash cycle yet?
Is the cotton white or dyed? (dyed cotton + stomach acid contact for hours = dye damage risk)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Gloves + scrape + cold flush is the time-sensitive trio; the baking-soda neutralize is the step that protects the fabric's dye from long-term acid fade. Padding involvement = pro.
